title: Use matrices to represent systems of equations
description: Systems of linear equations can be represented by a single matrix equation. Practice representing systems of 3 linear equations.
topic_path: Topic C: Systems of linear equations
Use matrices to represent systems of equations

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider this system of equations.

$\left\{\,\begin{align}20x+35y-10z&=15
\\\\16x+8y-4z&=12
\\\\3x-6y+9z&=18\end{align}\right.$

The system can be represented by the following matrix equation where $\bold A$ is a $3\times 3$ matrix and $\vec b$ is a 3D vector:

$\bold{A}\left[\begin{array}{c}
x \\\\
y \\\\
z \end{array} \right]=\vec{b}$

**Find $\bold A$ and $\vec b$.**

$\bold A$ | $\vec b$
:-: | :-:
[[☃ matrix 1]] | [[☃ matrix 2]]
hints:
###The key understanding

Let's say we have the following general system, where $\bold A_{i,j}$ and $b_i$ (where $i$ and $j$ go from $1$ to $3$) are coefficients:

$\begin{align}
\bold A_{1,1}\cdot x+\bold A_{1,2}\cdot y+\bold A_{1,3}\cdot z&=b_1
\\\\
\bold A_{2,1}\cdot x+\bold A_{2,2}\cdot y+\bold A_{2,3}\cdot z&=b_2
\\\\
\bold A_{3,1}\cdot x+\bold A_{3,2}\cdot y+\bold A_{3,3}\cdot z&=b_3
\end{align}$

Then this system can be represented by the following matrix equation:

$\left[\begin{array}{c}
\bold A_{1,1} & \bold A_{1,2} & \bold A_{1,3}
\\\\
\bold A_{2,1} & \bold A_{2,2} & \bold A_{2,3}
\\\\ 
\bold A_{3,1} & \bold A_{3,2} & \bold A_{3,3}
\end{array} \right]\left[\begin{array}{c}
x
\\\\
y 
\\\\
z
\end{array} \right]=\left[\begin{array}{c}
b_1
\\\\
b_2
\\\\ 
b_3
\end{array}\right]$ 

[[☃ explanation 1]]

### Finding the constant vector $\vec b$

The constant vector $\vec b$ is the three constant values on the right-hand side of each equation, in order:

$\vec{b}=\left[\begin{array}{c}
\goldE{15} \\\\
\goldE{12} \\\\
\goldE{18} \end{array} \right]$

In conclusion, this is the matrix equation that represents the system of equations. 

$\overbrace{\left[\begin{array}{c}
20 & 35 & -10 \\\\
16 &8 & -4 \\\\
3 & -6 & 9
\end{array} \right]}^{\large \bold A}\left[\begin{array}{c}
x
\\\\
y 
\\\\
z
\end{array} \right]=\overbrace{\left[\begin{array}{c}
15 \\\\
12 \\\\
18
\end{array}\right]}^{\large \vec b}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider this system of equations.

$\left\{\,\begin{align}10x+13y+15z&=4
\\\\12x-2y+5z&=-8
\\\\2x+2y-6z&=4\end{align}\right.$

The system can be represented by the following matrix equation where $\bold A$ is a $3\times 3$ matrix and $\vec b$ is a 3D vector:

$\bold{A}\left[\begin{array}{c}
x \\\\
y \\\\
z \end{array} \right]=\vec{b}$

**Find $\bold A$ and $\vec b$.**

$\bold A$ | $\vec b$
:-: | :-:
[[☃ matrix 1]] | [[☃ matrix 2]]
hints:
###The key understanding

Let's say we have the following general system, where $\bold A_{i,j}$ and $b_i$ (where $i$ and $j$ go from $1$ to $3$) are coefficients:

$\begin{align}
\bold A_{1,1}\cdot x+\bold A_{1,2}\cdot y+\bold A_{1,3}\cdot z&=b_1
\\\\
\bold A_{2,1}\cdot x+\bold A_{2,2}\cdot y+\bold A_{2,3}\cdot z&=b_2
\\\\
\bold A_{3,1}\cdot x+\bold A_{3,2}\cdot y+\bold A_{3,3}\cdot z&=b_3
\end{align}$

Then this system can be represented by the following matrix equation:

$\left[\begin{array}{c}
\bold A_{1,1} & \bold A_{1,2} & \bold A_{1,3}
\\\\
\bold A_{2,1} & \bold A_{2,2} & \bold A_{2,3}
\\\\ 
\bold A_{3,1} & \bold A_{3,2} & \bold A_{3,3}
\end{array} \right]\left[\begin{array}{c}
x
\\\\
y 
\\\\
z
\end{array} \right]=\left[\begin{array}{c}
b_1
\\\\
b_2
\\\\ 
b_3
\end{array}\right]$ 

[[☃ explanation 2]]

### Finding the constant vector $\vec b$

The constant vector $\vec b$ is the three constant values on the right-hand side of each equation, in order:

$\vec{b}=\left[\begin{array}{c}
\goldE{4} \\\\
\goldE{-8} \\\\
\goldE{4} \end{array} \right]$

In conclusion, this is the matrix equation that represents the system of equations. 

$\overbrace{\left[\begin{array}{c}
10 & 13 & 15 \\\\
12 & -2 & 5 \\\\
2 & 2& -6 
\end{array} \right]}^{\large \bold A}\left[\begin{array}{c}
x
\\\\
y 
\\\\
z
\end{array} \right]=\overbrace{\left[\begin{array}{c}
4 \\\\
-8 \\\\
4
\end{array}\right]}^{\large \vec b}$

3 & 5 & 9 \\\\
12 & 16 & 8

x
\\\\
y 
\\\\
z

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider this system of equations.

$\left\{\,\begin{align}2x+3y+z&=7
\\\\
-3x-9y+2z&=-4
\\\\
5x-y-7z&=12\end{align}\right.$

The system can be represented by the following matrix equation where $\bold A$ is a $3\times 3$ matrix and $\vec b$ is a 3D vector:

$\bold{A}\left[\begin{array}{c}
x \\\\
y \\\\
z \end{array} \right]=\vec{b}$

**Find $\bold A$ and $\vec b$.**

$\bold A$ | $\vec b$
:-: | :-:
[[☃ matrix 1]] | [[☃ matrix 2]]

\\\\ 
\bold A_{3,1} & \bold A_{3,2} & \bold A_{3,3}
\end{array} \right]\left[\begin{array}{c}
x
\\\\
y 
\\\\
z
\end{array} \right]=\left[\begin{array}{c}
b_1
\\\\
b_2
\\\\ 
b_3
\end{array}\right]$ 

[[☃ explanation 2]]

### Finding the constant vector $\vec b$

The constant vector $\vec b$ is the three constant values on the right-hand side of each equation, in order:

$\vec{b}=\left[\begin{array}{c}
\goldE{7} \\\\
\goldE{-4} \\\\
\goldE{12} \end{array} \right]$

In conclusion, this is the matrix equation that represents the system of equations. 

$\overbrace{\left[\begin{array}{c}
2 & 3 & 1 \\\\
-3 & -9 & 2 \\\\
5 & -1 & -7
\end{array} \right]}^{\large \bold A}\left[\begin{array}{c}
x
\\\\
y 
\\\\
z
\end{array} \right]=\overbrace{\left[\begin{array}{c}
7 \\\\
-4 \\\\
12 
\end{array}\right]}^{\large \vec b}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider this system of equations.

$\left\{\,\begin{align}12x+15y+2z&=19
\\\\x-8y+6z&=14
\\\\15x+12y-6z&=3\end{align}\right.$

The system can be represented by the following matrix equation where $\bold A$ is a $3\times 3$ matrix and $\vec b$ is a 3D vector:

$\bold{A}\left[\begin{array}{c}
x \\\\
y \\\\
z \end{array} \right]=\vec{b}$

**Find $\bold A$ and $\vec b$.**

$\bold A$ | $\vec b$
:-: | :-:
[[☃ matrix 1]] | [[☃ matrix 2]]
hints:
###The key understanding

Let's say we have the following general system, where $\bold A_{i,j}$ and $b_i$ (where $i$ and $j$ go from $1$ to $3$) are coefficients:

$\begin{align}
\bold A_{1,1}\cdot x+\bold A_{1,2}\cdot y+\bold A_{1,3}\cdot z&=b_1
\\\\
\bold A_{2,1}\cdot x+\bold A_{2,2}\cdot y+\bold A_{2,3}\cdot z&=b_2
\\\\
\bold A_{3,1}\cdot x+\bold A_{3,2}\cdot y+\bold A_{3,3}\cdot z&=b_3
\end{align}$

Then this system can be represented by the following matrix equation:

$\left[\begin{array}{c}
\bold A_{1,1} & \bold A_{1,2} & \bold A_{1,3}
\\\\
\bold A_{2,1} & \bold A_{2,2} & \bold A_{2,3}
\\\\ 
\bold A_{3,1} & \bold A_{3,2} & \bold A_{3,3}
\end{array} \right]\left[\begin{array}{c}
x
\\\\
y 
\\\\
z
\end{array} \right]=\left[\begin{array}{c}
b_1
\\\\
b_2
\\\\ 
b_3
\end{array}\right]$ 

[[☃ explanation 2]]

### Finding the constant vector $\vec b$

The constant vector $\vec b$ is the three constant values on the right-hand side of each equation, in order:

$\vec{b}=\left[\begin{array}{c}
\goldE{19} \\\\
\goldE{14} \\\\
\goldE{3} \end{array} \right]$

In conclusion, this is the matrix equation that represents the system of equations. 

$\overbrace{\left[\begin{array}{c}
12 & 15 & 2 \\\\
1 & -8 & 6 \\\\
15 & 12 & -6
\end{array} \right]}^{\large \bold A}\left[\begin{array}{c}
x
\\\\
y 
\\\\
z
\end{array} \right]=\overbrace{\left[\begin{array}{c}
19 \\\\
14 \\\\
3
\end{array}\right]}^{\large \vec b}$

### Finding the constant vector $\vec b$

The constant vector $\vec b$ is the three constant values on the right-hand side of each equation, in order:

$\vec{b}=\left[\begin{array}{c}
\goldE{22} \\\\
\goldE{24} \\\\
\goldE{-4} \end{array} \right]$

In conclusion, this is the matrix equation that represents the system of equations. 

$\overbrace{\left[\begin{array}{c}
6 & 5 & 0 \\\\
0 & -10 & 22 \\\\
4 & 0 & -6
\end{array} \right]}^{\large \bold A}\left[\begin{array}{c}
x
\\\\
y 
\\\\
z
\end{array} \right]=\overbrace{\left[\begin{array}{c}
22 \\\\
24 \\\\
-4 
\end{array}\right]}^{\large \vec b}$
